splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the timing requirements for actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability under ERISA. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing of assumption selection directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that actuaries may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, giving plans flexibility to use current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be fixed by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability about potential withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Sixth Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts can apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year and raises fundamental questions about judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, allowing courts to exercise jurisdiction over violations discovered after the original term would have expired.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and that judicially-created tolling impermissibly extends punishment beyond what Congress authorized.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) — which prohibits raising any claim previously presented — when bringing successive collateral attacks under § 2255. If it applies, federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement than § 2255(h) alone would impose.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, categorically barring claims that were previously presented.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners' § 2255 motions, so they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. The resolution affects whether candidates can challenge rules on mail-in voting, ballot counting deadlines, and other election administration practices before an election takes place, or whether such disputes must wait until after the election.

positions.label: Candidates have standing for pre-election challenges
positions.summary: Holds that candidates have Article III standing to challenge election rules before an election, based on competitive injury from allegedly unlawful voting procedures.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidates lack standing for pre-election challenges
positions.summary: Holds that candidates do not have Article III standing to challenge state election rules pre-election absent a more concrete and particularized injury.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to be met for disqualification) or disjunctive (requiring only one).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether a defendant is disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), or whether possessing any one criterion is disqualifying. This split leads to dramatically different sentences for defendants with identical criminal histories depending on where they are prosecuted.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria, reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as distributive or disjunctive in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the rule of lenity trumps deference to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations of ambiguous criminal provisions. The Third Circuit held that agency deference trumps lenity, while the Fourth, Fifth, Sixth, and Eleventh Circuits hold that lenity prevails. This split affects how criminal sentencing enhancements are construed nationwide.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over Auer/Kisor deference or Chevron deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal or sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through delegated interpretive power.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that deference to the Sentencing Commission's commentary or agency interpretation prevails over the rule of lenity in construing ambiguous sentencing guidelines or criminal provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) includes inchoate offenses such as conspiracy, attempt, and aiding and abetting, based on Application Note 1 to the commentary.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which adds conspiracy and attempt to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid authoritative interpretation of the guideline text. Some circuits defer to the commentary under Stinson, while others hold the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses and the commentary impermissibly expands it.

positions.label: Commentary validly includes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1's inclusion of conspiracy and attempt as controlled substance offenses is a valid interpretation of the guideline entitled to deference under Stinson v. United States.

positions.label: Guideline text excludes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that the text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline beyond its plain meaning, so it is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state controls a substance not listed on the federal schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' is limited to substances controlled under federal law, or whether it encompasses state-controlled substances not found on federal schedules. The split affects career-offender designations and base offense level calculations for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: State-controlled substances qualify
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' includes state law offenses even if the particular substance is not controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act, because the guideline text refers broadly to 'state law.'

positions.label: Only federally-controlled substances qualify
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines only if the substance at issue is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can contribute to a finding of 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether non-retroactive changes in law can be considered as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar reliance on non-retroactive legal changes, while others permit courts to consider them, at least in combination with other factors. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to address this, but some circuits have held that amendment exceeds the Commission's authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered, alone or in combination with other factors, as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, as this would circumvent Congress's decision not to make such changes retroactive.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert their Chapter 13 reorganization to a Chapter 7 liquidation in good faith under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtors. The Ninth Circuit held that such appreciation belongs to the estate, while other circuits have held it belongs to the debtors, creating a split with significant practical consequences for homeowners in bankruptcy.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), coupled with the broad scope of § 541(a), means post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtors
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A)'s reference to 'property of the estate, as of the date of filing' means post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtors, consistent with the statute's structure, policies, and legislative history.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to appeals from orders denying attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment in criminal cases.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case is governed by the civil appeal deadline (60 days for government cases) or the criminal appeal deadline (14 days). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because a Hyde Amendment motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, but noted a lopsided circuit split.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Manchester Farming Partnership

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment fee motion is part of the criminal case, so the criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly impose punitive sentences at supervised release revocation hearings, or whether revocation sentences must be limited to remedial purposes such as promoting compliance and deterring future violations.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the permissible purposes of sentences imposed upon revocation of supervised release. The Sixth Circuit allows district courts to expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release, while other circuits hold that revocation sentences must serve forward-looking goals rather than backward-looking punishment. This affects hundreds of revocation proceedings each year.

positions.label: Punishment is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may impose punitive sentences upon revocation of supervised release to sanction the defendant's violation of release conditions.

positions.label: Only remedial/deterrent purposes are permissible
positions.summary: Holds that the purpose of a revocation sentence is to sanction the breach of trust and promote future compliance, not to punish the underlying violation; revocation sentences must serve forward-looking remedial goals.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents referencing private medical treatments and diagnoses is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that orders denying motions to seal medical information are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of 'high order,' recognizing a potential split with circuits that have taken a narrower view of the collateral order doctrine's application to sealing orders. The First Circuit and D.C. Circuit appear to support immediate appealability, while other circuits have been more restrictive.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denial of a motion to seal medical records satisfies the collateral order doctrine because maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of high order that would be effectively unreviewable after final judgment.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal generally do not satisfy the collateral order doctrine because alternative remedies such as protective orders or post-judgment appeal can adequately vindicate privacy interests.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: In re City of New York

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether state tort claims against freight brokers for negligently selecting or monitoring motor carriers are preempted by the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prohibits states from enacting laws 'related to' the price, route, or service of a motor carrier or broker. This affects liability for trucking accidents arranged through brokers nationwide.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted because they are 'related to' the broker's services within the meaning of the FAAAA preemption clause.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA's preemption provision does not extend to state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent hiring or selection of motor carriers, as such claims do not regulate the broker's price, route, or service.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal prohibition on firearm possession by unlawful users of controlled substances, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen, circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by unlawful drug users is constitutional as applied to marijuana users. Some circuits have upheld the statute, while others have found it unconstitutional, creating divergent rules on gun rights for marijuana users across the country.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because historical tradition supports disarming those who use intoxicating substances.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming those who use controlled substances.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts may consider claims that overlap with habeas-type challenges (such as alleged trial errors, ineffective assistance of counsel, or legal innocence) when evaluating compassionate release motions. Some circuits permit such consideration as one factor in the analysis, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used as a substitute for habeas relief under § 2255.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider factors related to potential errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used to challenge the legality of a conviction or sentence, as such claims must be brought through habeas corpus under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FIFRA's labeling requirements preempt state tort claims alleging that a pesticide manufacturer should have included additional warnings on its label. This affects products liability litigation involving herbicides and pesticides nationwide, including high-profile Roundup litigation.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because allowing such claims would impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those required under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state failure-to-warn claims are not preempted by FIFRA because they parallel, rather than impose requirements 'in addition to or different from,' federal labeling requirements.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, exempting them from mandatory arbitration.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to construe the FAA's Section 1 exemption for transportation workers. The question of whether last-mile delivery workers who distribute goods to retail stores are 'transportation workers' engaged in interstate commerce has divided the circuits, with implications for millions of workers in logistics and distribution chains.

positions.label: Broad exemption — last-mile distributors qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who distribute goods that have traveled in interstate commerce, including last-mile delivery workers, are 'transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce' exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow exemption — last-mile distributors do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that the Section 1 exemption is limited to workers in the transportation industry who are directly engaged in the movement of goods across borders or state lines, not to workers who merely handle goods that previously traveled in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of the right to make and enforce contracts?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standard for § 1981 claims in hospitality and service contexts, particularly regarding what constitutes interference with the right to make and enforce contracts and the available remedies. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach against other circuits' frameworks.

positions.label: Narrower standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 claims require a showing of interference with an actual contractual relationship or attempted contract, applying a more restrictive view of what constitutes actionable discrimination.

positions.label: Broader standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 covers a broader range of discriminatory conduct in business establishments, including degraded service or hostile treatment that impairs the enjoyment of contracted services.
